en.extract:
The pygmy bushtit (Aegithalos exilis), also called the pygmy tit, is the smallest species of passerine bird. It is in the Aegithalidae family, containing the long-tailed tits. This drab-coloured bird is endemic to Indonesia, where it is only found in montane forests on the island of Java. It is a social songbird, living in small flocks, and a popular bird among bird watchers at Mt Gede and its adjacent botanical gardens. Relative to other members of the Aegithalidae family, it is not well studied and much remains unknown.


Taxonomy
The pygmy bushtit was first described by Dutch naturalist Coenraad Jacob Temminck in 1836 in the third volume of his book "Nouveau recueil de planches coloriées d'oiseaux". The pygmy tit was originally put in the monotypic genus Psaltria, until a genetic study in 2016 showed they are nested in the Aegithalos genus, with their closest relative being the black-throated bushtit (Aegithalos concinnu). Today, many books and articles still have the pygmy tit listed in the genus Psaltria.
The term Psaltria translates to "female harpist" in Latin. This could be a reference to the pygmy tits voice. The term Aegithalos was used by Aristotle to describe three species of European tits; the long-tailed tit (Aegithalos caudaus), the great tit (Parus major), and the European blue tit (Cyanistes caeruleus). Exilis is the  Latin word meaning "little" or "slender", a reference to the pygmy tits petite size.


Description
The pygmy bushtit is 85 millimetres (3.3 in) long, with about half of its length belonging to the tail. The bird has dull coloration with the head being a drab brown color with a cinnamon-tinged forehead. The upperparts and wings are a medium grey with a brownish tinge and the rump is a purer grey. Worn flight feathers may appear slightly paler than newer ones. The long tail is a dull grey-brown, with the outermost feathers slightly more pale at the tip. The throat and chin are a light grey with a pale pinkish-buff mottling and border. The breast and flank are greyish-brown, with a faint narrow breastband across the chest. The remaining underparts, including the underwing is a pale pinkish-buff colour. Both sexes look alike.

The eyes are a yellowish-white, sometimes brown or grey with age. The bill is short with a broad base and can be grey, dark brown or black in colour. The legs are yellow.
The pygmy bushtit weighs less than 5 grams (0.18 oz), making it smaller than many hummingbirds. The wings measure 40–49 millimetres (1.6–1.9 in) in males and 42–44 millimetres (1.7–1.7 in) in females. The tail measures 38–42 millimetres (1.5–1.7 in) in males and 33–39 millimetres (1.3–1.5 in) in females. The bill measures 6.3–8.9 millimetres (0.25–0.35 in). Juveniles remain undescribed but are presumed to be similar to adults.


Distribution and habitat
The pygmy bushtit is the only Indomalayan member of the Aegithalidae family. It is only found on the island of Java in Indonesia where it inhabits montane forests and plantations above 1,000 metres (3,300 ft), but can occasionally be seen as low as 830 metres (2,720 ft). They live on the edge of coniferous and open tree forests in the mountains of central and western Java. They are a non-migratory species that may move altitudinally.


Behaviour


Vocalization
The pygmy bushtit's voice is quite similar to many long-tailed tits and bushtits in its contact calls and lack of a territorial song. Most vocalizations are a combination of up to three elements: a high-pitched si or sisisi which can be prolonged becoming more of a silili, a high-pitched, raspy srrrr, and a soft chip, tchip, or, sip.
All members of the genus Aegithalos are very vocal due to their highly social nature. Communication is constant among flock members with a variety of soft, clipped monosyllable calls and short silvery trills. Calls can be quiet and subdued or loud and far-carrying. Both sexes emit quiet twittering and trilling sounds that are especially aggressive during copulation and encounters with conspecifics.


Food and feeding
The pygmy bushtit feeds primarily on insects including aphids (Aphidoidea) and caterpillars, as well as small spiders (Araneae). Plants such as berries or seeds are occasionally consumed by some long-tailed tits during the winter.

Members of the long-tails tit family forage actively in small flocks, often at low levels. The pygmy bushtit generally forages arboreally in the shrub layer or canopy, rarely visiting the ground. Prey is generally gleaned of branches, leaves and buds, rarely taken while in the air. They have been known to hang upside down from branches and to manipulate leaves to locate hidden prey.


Breeding
Little is known about pygmy tit reproduction. The pygmy bushtit has two breeding seasons: March through May and August through November. The nest is an 8–17 centimetre (3.1–6.7 in) tall 6–8.5 centimetre (2.4–3.3 in) wide pouch of leaves and grass lined with moss. The nest is suspended with a 2–3 centimetres (0.79–1.18 in) wide entrance hole near the top and inner chamber that is 3–5.5 centimetres (1.2–2.2 in) deep. The nest is similar to the nests of flowerpeckers (Dicaeidae).
The female tit lays a clutch of 2-3 eggs about 11.8–13.4 by 9.3–10.6 millimetres (0.46 in–0.53 in × 0.37 in–0.42 in) that are white with fine red spots.
Members of the Aegithalos family are generally monogamous, with both parents contributing to offspring care and many species utilizing relatives as helpers. It is not known if the pigmy tit utilizes these techniques. Nothing further is known about pygmy tit breeding biology.


Conservation status
The pygmy bushtit is not globally threatened and is currently listed as least concern under the IUCN redlist as of 2024. It is locally common and a restricted range species. The exact number of individuals is not known but its population trend is decreasing. The pygmy tits current threats are unknown, however the densely populated island of Java could faces issues as the mountain forests could be threatened by deforestation over time.


References

The '''pygmy bushtit (''Aegithalos exilis'')''', also called the '''pygmy tit''', is the smallest species of [[passerine]] bird. It is in the [[Aegithalidae]] family, containing the long-tailed tits. This drab-coloured bird is endemic to Indonesia, where it is only found in montane forests on the island of Java. It is a social songbird, living in small flocks, and a popular bird among bird watchers at [[Mount Gede|Mt Gede]] and its adjacent botanical gardens. Relative to other members of the Aegithalidae family, it is not well studied and much remains unknown.

== Taxonomy ==
The pygmy bushtit was first described by Dutch naturalist [[Coenraad Jacob Temminck]] in 1836 in the third volume of his book "Nouveau recueil de planches coloriées d'oiseaux".<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal |last=Harrap |first=Simon |date=2022 |title=Pygmy Tit (Aegithalos exilis), version 1.1 |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/pygtit1/cur/introduction |journal=Birds of the World |language=en |doi=10.2173/bow.pygtit1.01.1 |issn=2771-3105|url-access=subscription }}</ref> The pygmy tit was originally put in the monotypic genus ''Psaltria'', until a genetic study in 2016 showed they are nested in the ''[[Aegithalos]]'' genus, with their closest relative being the [[black-throated bushtit]] (''Aegithalos concinnu)''.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Johansson |first=Ulf S. |last2=Ericson |first2=Per G. P. |last3=Fjeldså |first3=Jon |last4=Irestedt |first4=Martin |date=2016 |title=The phylogenetic position of the world's smallest passerine, the Pygmy Bushtit Psaltria exilis |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1111/ibi.12377 |journal=Ibis |language=en |volume=158 |issue=3 |pages=519–529 |doi=10.1111/ibi.12377 |issn=1474-919X |url-access=subscription}}</ref><ref name=":1" /> Today, many books and articles still have the pygmy tit listed in the genus ''Psaltri''a.

The term ''Psaltria'' translates to "female harpist" in Latin.<ref name=":7" /> This could be a reference to the pygmy tits voice. The term ''Aegithalos'' was used by [[Aristotle]] to describe three species of European tits; the [[long-tailed tit]] (''Aegithalos caudaus''), the [[great tit]] (''Parus major''), and the [[Eurasian blue tit|European blue tit]] (''Cyanistes caeruleus'').<ref name=":7">{{Cite journal |date=2011-05-03 |title=The Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names: From Aalge to Zusii (New edition) |url=https://doi.org/10.1108/09504121111134070 |journal=Reference Reviews |volume=25 |issue=4 |pages=41–42 |doi=10.1108/09504121111134070 |issn=0950-4125|url-access=subscription }}</ref> ''Exilis'' is the  Latin word meaning "little" or "slender", a reference to the pygmy tits petite size.<ref name=":7" />

== Description ==
The pygmy bushtit is 85 millimetres (3.3 in) long, with about half of its length belonging to the tail.<ref name=":3">{{Cite book |last=Harrap |first=Simon |title=Tits, Nuthatches and Treecreepers |date=2010 |publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing |isbn=978-1-4081-3518-1 |series=Helm Identification Guides |location=London}}</ref><ref name=":4">{{Cite book |last=Temminck |first=C.J. |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/item/112979 |title=Nouveau recueil de planches coloriées d'oiseaux : pour servir de suite et de complément aux planches enluminées de Buffon, édition in-folio et in-4⁰ de l'Imprimerie royale, 1770 |last2=Temminck |first2=C. J. |last3=Buffon |first3=Georges Louis Leclerc |last4=Huet |first4=Nicholas |last5=Laugier de Chartrouse |first5=Guillaume Michel Jerôme Meiffren |last6=Prêtre |first6=J. G. |date=1838 |publisher=Chez Legras Imbert et Comp |volume=v.3 (1838) |location=A Strasbourgh}}</ref> The bird has dull coloration with the head being a drab brown color with a cinnamon-tinged forehead. The upperparts and wings are a medium grey with a brownish tinge and the rump is a purer grey. Worn flight feathers may appear slightly paler than newer ones. The long tail is a dull grey-brown, with the outermost feathers slightly more pale at the tip. The throat and chin are a light grey with a pale pinkish-buff mottling and border. The breast and flank are greyish-brown, with a faint narrow breastband across the chest. The remaining underparts, including the underwing is a pale pinkish-buff colour.<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":4" /> Both sexes look alike.

The eyes are a yellowish-white, sometimes brown or grey with age. The bill is short with a broad base and can be grey, dark brown or black in colour. The legs are yellow.<ref name=":3" />[[File:Psaltria exilis 1838.jpg|thumb|Original drawing of ''Psaltria exilis'', 1838]]

The pygmy bushtit weighs less than 5 grams (0.18 oz), making it smaller than many [[hummingbird]]s.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Schmitt |first=C. Jonathan |last2=Edwards |first2=Scott V. |date=2022-10-24 |title=Passerine birds |url=https://www.cell.com/current-biology/abstract/S0960-9822(22)01383-5 |journal=Current Biology |language=English |volume=32 |issue=20 |pages=R1149–R1154 |doi=10.1016/j.cub.2022.08.061 |issn=0960-9822 |pmid=36283383}}</ref> The wings measure 40–49 millimetres (1.6–1.9 in) in males and 42–44 millimetres (1.7–1.7 in) in females. The tail measures 38–42 millimetres (1.5–1.7 in) in males and 33–39 millimetres (1.3–1.5 in) in females. The bill measures 6.3–8.9 millimetres (0.25–0.35 in).<ref name=":3" /> Juveniles remain undescribed but are presumed to be similar to adults.<ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last=Hoyo |first=Josep de |title=Handbook of the Birds of the World |last2=Elliott |first2=Andrew |last3=Christie |first3=David |publisher=Lynx Edicions |year=2008 |isbn=978-84-96553-45-3 |volume=13: Penduline-tits to Shrikes |pages=76–101}}</ref>

== Distribution and habitat ==
The pygmy bushtit is the only [[Indomalayan realm|Indomalayan]] member of the ''Aegithalidae'' family.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Päckert |first=Martin |last2=Martens |first2=Jochen |last3=Sun |first3=Yue-Hua |date=2010-06-01 |title=Phylogeny of long-tailed tits and allies inferred from mitochondrial and nuclear markers (Aves: Passeriformes, Aegithalidae) |url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1055790310000266 |journal=Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution |volume=55 |issue=3 |pages=952–967 |doi=10.1016/j.ympev.2010.01.024 |issn=1055-7903|url-access=subscription }}</ref> It is only found on the island of [[Java]] in Indonesia where it inhabits montane forests and plantations above 1,000 metres (3,300 ft), but can occasionally be seen as low as 830 metres (2,720 ft).<ref name=":3" /> They live on the edge of coniferous and open tree forests in the mountains of central and western Java. They are a non-migratory species that may move altitudinally.<ref name=":2" /><ref name=":3" />

== Behaviour ==

=== Vocalization ===
The pygmy bushtit's voice is quite similar to many long-tailed tits and bushtits in its contact calls and lack of a territorial song.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":3" /> Most vocalizations are a combination of up to three elements: a high-pitched ''{{not a typo|si}}'' or ''{{not a typo|sisisi}}'' which can be prolonged becoming more of a ''{{not a typo|silili}}'', a high-pitched, raspy ''{{not a typo|srrrr}}'', and a soft ''{{not a typo|chip, tchip}}'', or, ''{{not a typo|sip}}''.<ref name=":3" />

All members of the genus ''Aegithalos'' are very vocal due to their highly social nature. Communication is constant among flock members with a variety of soft, clipped monosyllable calls and short silvery trills. Calls can be quiet and subdued or loud and far-carrying. Both sexes emit quiet twittering and trilling sounds that are especially aggressive during copulation and encounters with conspecifics.<ref name=":2" />

=== Food and feeding ===
The pygmy bushtit feeds primarily on [[insect]]s including [[aphid]]s (''Aphidoidea)'' and [[caterpillar]]s, as well as small [[spider]]s (''Araneae''). Plants such as berries or seeds are occasionally consumed by some long-tailed tits during the winter.<ref name=":2" />

Members of the long-tails tit family forage actively in small flocks, often at low levels. The pygmy bushtit generally forages [[Arboreal locomotion|arboreally]] in the shrub layer or canopy, rarely visiting the ground.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":3" /><ref name=":5">{{Citation |title=Family Aegithalidae: long-tailed tits |work=The Birds of Africa |url=https://doi.org/10.5040/9781472926975.part-016 |access-date=2025-10-24 |publisher=Academic Press}}</ref> Prey is generally [[Gleaning (birds)|gleaned]] of branches, leaves and buds, rarely taken while in the air. They have been known to hang upside down from branches and to manipulate leaves to locate hidden prey.<ref name=":5" />[[File:Pygmy Tit - Cibodas Bot.Gardens MG 3731 (29030183943).jpg|thumb|Pygmy Bushtit]]

=== Breeding ===
Little is known about pygmy tit reproduction. The pygmy bushtit has two breeding seasons: March through May and August through November. The nest is an 8–17 centimetre (3.1–6.7 in) tall 6–8.5 centimetre (2.4–3.3 in) wide pouch of leaves and grass lined with moss. The nest is suspended with a 2–3 centimetres (0.79–1.18 in) wide entrance hole near the top and inner chamber that is 3–5.5 centimetres (1.2–2.2 in) deep. The nest is similar to the nests of [[flowerpecker]]s (''Dicaeidae'').<ref name=":2" /><ref name=":1" />

The female tit lays a clutch of 2-3 eggs about 11.8–13.4 by 9.3–10.6 millimetres (0.46 in–0.53 in × 0.37 in–0.42 in) that are white with fine red spots.<ref name=":2" />

Members of the ''Aegithalos'' family are generally monogamous, with both parents contributing to offspring care and many species utilizing relatives as helpers.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Bird Aegithalidae - Tit-warblers, Bush Tits & Long-tailed Tit |url=https://fatbirder.com/ornithology/aegithalidae-bush-tits/ |access-date=2025-10-27 |website=Fat Birder |language=en-GB}}</ref> It is not known if the pigmy tit utilizes these techniques. Nothing further is known about pygmy tit breeding biology.

== Conservation status ==
The pygmy bushtit is not globally threatened and is currently listed as least concern under the [[IUCN Red List|IUCN redlist]] as of 2024.<ref name=":6">{{Cite report |url=https://www.iucnredlist.org/species/22712032/94316107 |title=Psaltria exilis: BirdLife International: The IUCN Red List of Threatened Species 2016: e.T22712032A94316107 |last=IUCN |date=2016-10-01 |publisher=International Union for Conservation of Nature |doi=10.2305/iucn.uk.2016-3.rlts.t22712032a94316107.en |language=en}}</ref> It is locally common and a restricted range species. The exact number of individuals is not known but its population trend is decreasing. The pygmy tits current threats are unknown, however the densely populated island of Java could faces issues as the mountain forests could be threatened by deforestation over time.<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":6" />
